message:
perf(raptorq): widen GF(2^8) inner loops for higher throughput

Increase the processing width of the three GF(2^8) bulk operations to
exploit instruction-level parallelism and reduce loop overhead on modern
CPUs with wide pipelines and store buffers.

gf256_add_slice (XOR accumulation):
- Widen main loop from 8-byte (1×u64) to 32-byte (4×u64) per iteration,
  processing 4 independent XOR operations that the CPU can pipeline
  without data dependencies between lanes.
- Add an 8-byte (1×u64) intermediate tail loop before the scalar tail
  to handle the 8–24 byte remainder without falling all the way back
  to byte-at-a-time.
- Replace .expect("8 bytes") with .unwrap() in the tail since the
  chunks_exact iterator guarantees the length.

mul_with_table_wide (in-place GF multiply):
- Widen from 8-byte to 16-byte batches (16 table lookups per iteration).
  Since each lookup is independent, doubling the batch width doubles
  the available ILP for the CPU's load/store units.
- Scalar tail handles the 0–15 byte remainder.

addmul_with_table_wide (XOR-accumulate GF multiply):
- Widen from 8-byte to 16-byte batches (2×u64 XOR per iteration).
  Split the 16-byte result into lo/hi u64 halves for the XOR step,
  maintaining the wide-store pattern while doubling lookup parallelism.
- Both the table lookup (16 independent loads) and the XOR accumulation
  (2 independent u64 ops) benefit from the wider batch.

These functions are the hot inner loops for RaptorQ encode/decode
(Gaussian elimination, repair symbol generation). The widening is
purely mechanical — no algorithmic changes, no unsafe code, and all
existing tests continue to pass since the operations are associative
and order-independent.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>
